past_medical_history: Hemithyroidectomy for thyroid follicular carcinoma (pT2N0M0) 8 years ago, followed by radioactive iodine ablation; most recent thyroglobulin level 6 months ago was undetectable and surveillance imaging negative
Hypertension, well controlled on amlodipine for 12 years
Osteopenia diagnosed 3 years ago on DEXA scan

Chronic exercise-related dyspnea and occasional retrosternal discomfort, evaluated by cardiology 4 months ago with normal echocardiogram, normal stress test, and normal ambulatory blood pressure monitoring
Dysphagia evaluated by gastroenterology 3 months ago with normal upper endoscopy; attributed to functional dysphagia
family_history: Mother: breast cancer diagnosed at age 62, died at age 74
Father: prostate cancer, died at age 79
Sister: thyroid nodules, benign on biopsy
No family history of brain tumors or neurological diseases

social_history.alcohol: Rare, glass of wine on special occasions
social_history.living_situation: Lives alone in a single-story house; widowed 3 years ago; daughter lives nearby and visits regularly
social_history.functional_status: WHO performance status 0 prior to current presentation; independent in all ADLs and IADLs

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, and time. Appears mildly anxious. Speech is fluent with normal rate but occasional word-finding pauses, which the daughter states are new over the past several weeks. No paraphasic errors on spontaneous speech. Confrontation naming shows hesitation on low-frequency items (2 errors on 15-item Boston Naming Test subset). Able to follow complex three-step commands. Registers 3/3 words but recalls only 1/3 at five minutes with 1 additional on cueing. Attention intact on serial 7s and digit span forward (5 digits). Montreal Cognitive Assessment estimated at 23/30 with deficits in delayed recall, abstraction, and visuospatial domains.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal round and reactive, 3 mm bilaterally. Visual fields full to confrontation. No papilledema on fundoscopic examination. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact bilaterally. Mild flattening of the left nasolabial fold noted, subtle. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius full bilaterally. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal throughout. Tone mildly increased in left upper extremity compared to right. Strength: right upper and lower extremities 5/5 throughout. Left upper extremity: shoulder abduction 4+/5, grip strength 4+/5, otherwise 5/5. Left lower extremity 5/5 throughout. Subtle left pronator drift present on extended arm testing.
neurological_exam.sensory: Intact to light touch and pinprick bilaterally. Vibration mildly reduced at both great toes (symmetric, age-appropriate). Proprioception intact. Graphesthesia intact bilaterally. No extinction to double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Right biceps 2+, left biceps 2+. Right triceps 2+, left triceps 3+. Brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally. Right patellar 2+, left patellar 3+. Achilles 1+ bilaterally. Left plantar response equivocal (neither clearly flexor nor extensor). Right plantar response flexor.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact on right, mild terminal dysmetria on left. Heel-to-shin testing intact bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements slightly slowed on the left compared to right.
neurological_exam.gait: Mildly wide-based gait. Slight left leg circumduction noted. Tandem gait mildly unsteady with one misstep. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: The patient's daughter reports a 6-week history of progressive personality change: the patient has become more apathetic, less interested in her grandchildren, and has on two occasions left the stove on unattended. She had one episode of involuntary jerking of the left hand lasting approximately 30 seconds witnessed by the daughter 5 days ago, which prompted the current evaluation. No loss of consciousness during this episode.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive personality changes, word-finding difficulty, and one episode of involuntary left hand jerking over the past 6 weeks
patient.history_present_illness: This 68-year-old right-handed woman presents to the neurology service following a referral from her primary care physician for evaluation of progressive cognitive and behavioral changes. Her daughter, who is the primary informant, reports that beginning approximately six weeks ago the patient developed a noticeable change in personality. She had previously been an active, socially engaged retired schoolteacher who volunteered at her local library and regularly attended a weekly book club. Over the past six weeks she has gradually withdrawn from these activities, become apathetic, and shows diminished interest in her grandchildren who previously were the center of her life. The daughter describes her mother as seeming 'flat' and 'not herself,' with reduced spontaneous conversation and increasing difficulty finding words during speech. On two separate occasions in the past three weeks, the patient left the stove on unattended, something she had never done before. She has also missed a scheduled doctor's appointment and forgotten to take her medications on several days, which is entirely out of character for a woman who previously managed her own medical care meticulously. The patient herself is somewhat dismissive of these changes, attributing them to grief over her husband's death three years ago and general aging. However, her daughter notes that these changes are distinctly different from the period of bereavement that followed her husband's death, during which the patient was sad but remained cognitively sharp and functionally independent. Five days prior to presentation, the daughter witnessed an episode where the patient's left hand began jerking involuntarily for approximately 30 seconds. The patient appeared aware during the episode and commented that her hand was 'doing something strange.' There was no loss of consciousness, no generalized convulsive activity, and no postictal confusion. She has also reported mild intermittent headaches over the past two to three weeks, predominantly on the right side, worse in the morning, and partially responsive to acetaminophen. She denies nausea, vomiting, or visual changes. Of significant concern in her history is a prior diagnosis of thyroid follicular carcinoma eight years ago, treated with hemithyroidectomy and radioactive iodine ablation. She has been followed regularly by oncology, with the most recent surveillance thyroglobulin level six months ago being undetectable and a recent CT of the neck showing no evidence of recurrence. Four months ago she underwent a comprehensive cardiologic workup for exercise-related dyspnea and occasional retrosternal discomfort; all studies were unremarkable. She was also evaluated by gastroenterology three months ago for dysphagia, with a normal upper endoscopy. Her current medications include levothyroxine, amlodipine, calcium carbonate with vitamin D, and low-dose aspirin.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 8 Hz alpha rhythm bilaterally, mildly slow for age, reactive to eye opening
background.asymmetry: Mild amplitude attenuation over the right hemisphere, difficult to lateralize precisely

findings.morphology: Polymorphic, moderate amplitude, intermittent bursts lasting 2-5 seconds
findings.state: Present during wakefulness, increases during drowsiness

findings.morphology: Broad-based, moderate amplitude, not clearly epileptiform vs. artifact

artifacts.description: Intermittent electrode pop artifact at F8, partially obscuring right frontotemporal activity
Bilateral temporal muscle artifact during periods of jaw clenching

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Diffuse background slowing increased symmetrically during hyperventilation. No lateralizing or localizing changes.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Symmetric photic driving at 8-14 Hz. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG for a 68-year-old female showing mild diffuse background slowing and intermittent right temporal focal slowing. Equivocal sharp-wave transients in the right centroparietal region are of uncertain significance and cannot be reliably classified as epileptiform discharges. The generalized slowing is nonspecific and may reflect an age-related neurodegenerative process, metabolic disturbance, or diffuse structural pathology. The right temporal focal slowing raises the possibility of an underlying structural lesion but is insufficient for definitive localization. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Routine 40-minute recording. Electrode artifact at F8 partially limits interpretation of right frontotemporal activity. Sleep was not achieved during this recording. Extended or ambulatory EEG monitoring may be beneficial if seizures are suspected.

findings.location: Right temporal lobe, involving the superior and middle temporal gyri, extending posteriorly into the temporoparietal junction and medially into the insula

signal_characteristics.T1: Predominantly hypointense with heterogeneous internal signal; no definite central necrosis identified
signal_characteristics.T2: Heterogeneously hyperintense with surrounding hyperintense signal extending into the right temporal and parietal white matter
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Extensive FLAIR hyperintensity involving the right temporal lobe, extending along white matter tracts into the right parietal lobe and posterior insula. The FLAIR abnormality extends well beyond the areas of enhancement.
signal_characteristics.DWI: Patchy areas of restricted diffusion peripherally, predominantly at the posterior margin; no central restricted diffusion
signal_characteristics.post_contrast: Patchy, irregular enhancement predominantly at the posterior and inferior margins of the lesion. No classic complete ring enhancement. Several small foci of punctate enhancement scattered within the mass. The anterior and medial portions of the lesion show minimal to no enhancement.

findings.mass_effect: Mild effacement of the right temporal horn of the lateral ventricle. Mild rightward-to-leftward midline shift measuring approximately 3 mm at the level of the septum pellucidum. No uncal herniation.
findings.borders: Ill-defined, infiltrative margins without a clear capsule or distinct boundary from surrounding brain parenchyma

findings.location: Scattered bilateral periventricular and subcortical white matter

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Multiple small punctate and confluent areas of FLAIR hyperintensity bilaterally, consistent with chronic small vessel ischemic changes
signal_characteristics.post_contrast: No enhancement of these white matter lesions

additional_observations: No additional suspicious enhancing lesions elsewhere in the brain
No hydrocephalus beyond mild temporal horn effacement
Right mastoid effusion noted, likely incidental
mri.impression: Large infiltrative mass in the right temporal lobe extending into the temporoparietal junction and insula, measuring 4.2 cm with extensive surrounding FLAIR abnormality, patchy irregular enhancement, and mild mass effect. The imaging appearance is diagnostically challenging. The predominantly non-enhancing, infiltrative pattern with extensive FLAIR abnormality could suggest a lower-grade glioma (WHO grade 2-3), but the patchy foci of enhancement, peripheral restricted diffusion, and mass effect raise concern for higher-grade transformation or an anaplastic component. In the clinical context of a prior thyroid follicular carcinoma, an unusual solitary metastatic lesion must also be considered, although the infiltrative margins are atypical for metastasis. CNS lymphoma is possible but the enhancement pattern is atypical. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings: Nonspecific ST-T wave changes in leads V4-V6
Voltage criteria for left ventricular hypertrophy not met
ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm at 76 bpm with nonspecific ST-T wave changes. No evidence of acute ischemia or arrhythmia.

findings.type: Perfusion imaging of right temporal mass
findings.location: Right temporal lobe mass with extension to temporoparietal junction

signal_characteristics.rCBV_enhancing_regions: Markedly elevated (rCBV ratio 3.4 relative to contralateral normal-appearing white matter) in the patchy enhancing foci at the posterior and inferior margins
signal_characteristics.rCBV_non_enhancing_core: Mildly elevated (rCBV ratio 1.6) in the non-enhancing central portions, above what would be expected for low-grade glioma or edema
signal_characteristics.rCBV_perilesional_FLAIR: Elevated (rCBV ratio 1.8) in the perilesional FLAIR-abnormal tissue, suggestive of tumor infiltration beyond the visible mass boundaries
signal_characteristics.permeability: Elevated Ktrans in areas of enhancement, consistent with blood-brain barrier disruption and neoangiogenesis

findings.borders: Perfusion abnormality extends beyond the margins of contrast enhancement into FLAIR-abnormal tissue

additional_observations: The markedly elevated rCBV (3.4) in enhancing areas far exceeds the threshold of 1.75 used to distinguish high-grade from low-grade gliomas, indicating significant neoangiogenesis
The perfusion pattern is inconsistent with metastatic disease, which typically shows elevated rCBV confined to the enhancing component with normal perfusion in surrounding edema
output.impression: MR perfusion imaging demonstrates markedly elevated cerebral blood volume (rCBV 3.4) in the enhancing components and mildly elevated rCBV (1.6) in non-enhancing portions of the right temporal mass. The elevated perfusion in non-enhancing regions indicates that the tumor grade is higher than the conventional MRI enhancement pattern would suggest, a pattern seen in glioblastomas that present with an initially non-enhancing or minimally enhancing appearance. The perfusion characteristics argue against metastatic disease and low-grade glioma. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.type: MR spectroscopy of right temporal mass
findings.location: Right temporal lobe lesion; multivoxel spectroscopy with voxels placed in enhancing regions, non-enhancing tumor core, and perilesional tissue

signal_characteristics.non_enhancing_core_lipid_lactate: Mild lipid peak, moderate lactate peak
signal_characteristics.perilesional_tissue: Mildly elevated choline with reduced NAA (Cho/NAA ratio 1.9), indicating tumor infiltration beyond visible lesion boundaries
signal_characteristics.myoinositol: Reduced in tumor core, normal in perilesional tissue

additional_observations: Critically, the NON-ENHANCING portion of the tumor also shows elevated choline and reduced NAA, confirming that the tumor is infiltrating beyond the areas of enhancement — a hallmark of diffuse gliomas and inconsistent with metastatic disease

Metastatic lesions typically show abnormal spectra only within the enhancing lesion, with normal spectra in surrounding edema — the finding of abnormal spectra in non-enhancing regions effectively excludes metastasis
output.impression: See findings above. Clinical correlation recommended.

CT Chest Abdomen Pelvis.value: Clear lung fields. No pulmonary nodules or masses. No pleural effusion. Two small calcified granulomas in the right lower lobe, consistent with prior granulomatous disease (incidental).
Right hemithyroidectomy bed shows expected post-surgical changes. No soft tissue mass or abnormal enhancement in the surgical bed. Left thyroid lobe remnant appears unremarkable.
No pathologically enlarged cervical lymph nodes. Several subcentimeter benign-appearing lymph nodes.
No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. Heart size normal. Mild coronary artery calcifications.
Homogeneous enhancement, no focal hepatic lesions.
Normal size bilaterally, no masses. No hydronephrosis.
No suspicious lytic or blastic osseous lesions. Mild degenerative changes of the lumbar spine.

Neuropathology.value: Densely cellular astrocytic neoplasm with diffusely infiltrating margins, marked nuclear atypia and pleomorphism, brisk mitotic activity (12 mitoses per 10 HPF), florid microvascular proliferation, and geographic areas of palisading necrosis. WHO grade 4 glioblastoma.
Combined whole chromosome 7 gain and chromosome 10 loss detected

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Histology Densely cellular astrocytic neoplasm with diffusely infiltrating margins, marked nuclear atypia and pleomorphism, brisk mitotic activity (12 mitoses per 10 HPF), florid microvascular proliferation, and geographic areas of palisading necrosis. WHO grade 4 glioblastoma.  (H), MGMT promoter methylation Methylated  (H), TERT promoter mutation Present (C228T)  (H), EGFR amplification Present (FISH)  (H), +7/-10 chromosome Combined whole chromosome 7 gain and chromosome 10 loss detected  (H), Ki-67 proliferation index 40%  (H), GFAP immunohistochemistry Positive, diffuse  (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Histology: Glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, WHO grade 4 with microvascular proliferation and palisading necrosis (H)

findings.type: Functional MRI for pre-surgical language and motor mapping
findings.location: Right temporal lobe lesion in relation to eloquent cortex

signal_characteristics.wernicke_area: Left Wernicke area activation normal and robust. Mild activation also noted in right superior temporal gyrus (bilateral language representation), partially overlapping with the anterior margin of the tumor.
signal_characteristics.broca_area: Left Broca area activation robust and appropriately lateralized.
signal_characteristics.language_laterality_index: Left-dominant but mildly bilateral (LI = 0.72). Some right hemispheric language activation overlaps with the anterior tumor margin.
signal_characteristics.motor_cortex: Right primary motor cortex activation normal, no displacement by tumor.
signal_characteristics.auditory_cortex: Right primary auditory cortex (Heschl gyrus) is partially involved by the medial extent of the tumor

findings.mass_effect: Tumor displaces right superior temporal sulcus posteriorly
findings.borders: Anterior margin of tumor approaches areas of right hemispheric language co-activation

additional_observations: Language is predominantly left-lateralized (LI 0.72) but with mild bilateral representation, which is not uncommon in older females
The partial overlap of right hemispheric language co-activation with the anterior tumor margin raises the possibility of language deficits with aggressive anterior resection
Right auditory cortex involvement may contribute to the patient's reported difficulty with speech comprehension in noisy environments
DTI tractography shows the right arcuate fasciculus is displaced medially by the tumor but remains intact
output.impression: Functional MRI demonstrates predominantly left-lateralized language function (LI 0.72) with mild bilateral representation. Right primary auditory cortex is partially involved by the tumor. Motor cortex is not at risk. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.query: thyroid follicular carcinoma brain metastasis imaging characteristics

results.title: Brain metastases from thyroid carcinoma: a systematic review
results.key_finding: Brain metastases from differentiated thyroid carcinoma are rare (0.15-1.3% of patients). They typically present as well-circumscribed, spherical, hypervascular lesions with homogeneous enhancement. Follicular subtype metastasizes to brain more frequently than papillary subtype. Median interval from thyroid cancer diagnosis to brain metastasis is 4.5 years.

results.title: Differentiating primary brain tumors from metastases: the role of advanced MRI techniques
results.key_finding: Key imaging distinctions: metastases show elevated perfusion confined to the enhancing lesion with normal perfusion in surrounding edema (vasogenic), while gliomas show elevated perfusion extending into perilesional tissue (infiltrative tumor). MR spectroscopy showing metabolic abnormalities in non-enhancing perilesional tissue is highly specific for primary glioma over metastasis.

results.title: Thyroglobulin elevation after hemithyroidectomy: clinical significance
results.key_finding: After hemithyroidectomy (as opposed to total thyroidectomy), detectable thyroglobulin levels up to 5-10 ng/mL may reflect residual normal thyroid tissue rather than cancer recurrence. Rising thyroglobulin trends over serial measurements are more concerning than a single elevated value. Clinical context and imaging correlation are essential.

output.summary: Brain metastases from differentiated thyroid carcinoma (particularly follicular subtype) are uncommon but documented, occurring in 0.15-1.3% of patients. They present as well-circumscribed, spherical, hypervascular lesions distinct from the infiltrative pattern characteristic of primary gliomas. Advanced MRI techniques (perfusion showing elevated rCBV only within the enhancing lesion, not in perilesional tissue) and MR spectroscopy (metabolic abnormalities confined to the enhancing lesion) are highly effective at distinguishing metastases from primary brain tumors. Importantly, thyroglobulin levels up to 5-10 ng/mL after hemithyroidectomy may reflect residual normal thyroid tissue and do not necessarily indicate cancer recurrence.

output.proposed: Temozolomide 75 mg/m2 daily during radiation (Stupp protocol)
interactions: Levothyroxine: No direct pharmacokinetic interaction. However, temozolomide-related nausea/vomiting may affect levothyroxine absorption. Monitor TSH levels during treatment.
Amlodipine: No significant interaction with temozolomide.
Aspirin 81 mg: Monitor for additive myelosuppression risk (thrombocytopenia from temozolomide plus antiplatelet effect). Consider holding aspirin if platelet count drops below 50,000.
Calcium carbonate: May theoretically affect temozolomide absorption if taken simultaneously. Separate administration by 2 hours.
contraindications: Severe hepatic or renal impairment (not applicable — patient has normal hepatic and renal function)
Known hypersensitivity to temozolomide or dacarbazine
warnings: Risk of myelosuppression, particularly lymphopenia and thrombocytopenia — requires weekly CBC monitoring during concurrent chemoradiation

Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia prophylaxis required due to treatment-induced lymphopenia (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is first-line but patient has penicillin allergy; note: TMP-SMX is a sulfonamide — must verify whether patient's penicillin allergy extends to sulfonamides; if sulfonamide-allergic, use dapsone or atovaquone)
Teratogenic: not applicable given patient age
alternatives: Bevacizumab — for recurrence or as adjunct in selected cases
Tumor treating fields (TTFields/Optune) — as adjunct to maintenance temozolomide

Thyroid Ultrasound.value: Post-surgical changes consistent with prior right hemithyroidectomy. No suspicious soft tissue mass in the surgical bed.

Left thyroid lobe measures 4.2 x 1.8 x 1.5 cm, normal in size. Heterogeneous echotexture with two small colloid cysts (3 mm and 5 mm), benign appearing. No suspicious solid nodules. No calcifications.
Multiple benign-appearing lymph nodes in levels II-IV bilaterally, all subcentimeter with preserved fatty hila. No suspicious lymphadenopathy.

differential.likelihood: Low-moderate (initially the leading alternative due to cancer history)
differential.key_distinguishing: History of thyroid follicular carcinoma and mildly elevated thyroglobulin make metastasis an important consideration. However, thyroid cancer brain metastases are rare and typically present as well-circumscribed, spherical, hypervascular lesions — not as infiltrative masses. MR perfusion shows elevated rCBV in perilesional tissue (infiltrative glioma pattern), MR spectroscopy shows metabolic abnormalities extending beyond enhancement (glioma pattern), CT body imaging shows no thyroid cancer recurrence, thyroid ultrasound shows residual thyroid tissue explaining the thyroglobulin elevation, and biopsy is TTF-1/PAX8 negative definitively excluding thyroid origin.

The predominantly non-enhancing infiltrative pattern initially suggests a lower-grade glioma. However, elevated rCBV in non-enhancing regions, high proliferation index (Ki-67 40%), and histological features of microvascular proliferation and palisading necrosis confirm WHO grade 4 (glioblastoma).

CNS lymphoma typically shows homogeneous enhancement (not patchy irregular enhancement), periventricular predilection, and dramatic response to corticosteroids. Patient is immunocompetent. The infiltrative pattern with patchy enhancement is atypical for lymphoma.

No primary extracranial malignancy identified on CT chest/abdomen/pelvis. Infiltrative growth pattern inconsistent with typical metastatic morphology. Family history of breast cancer could raise concern but the imaging pattern excludes metastasis.

Although the initial MRI appearance with predominantly non-enhancing infiltrative pattern could suggest low-grade glioma, the markedly elevated rCBV, high cellularity, mitotic activity, microvascular proliferation, and palisading necrosis on biopsy definitively exclude a low-grade tumor.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain comprehensive laboratory panel including CBC, BMP, hepatic panel, coagulation studies, thyroid function (TSH, free T4), thyroglobulin with anti-Tg antibodies, and LDH
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Largely normal labs with critically elevated thyroglobulin level creating diagnostic confusion about possible thyroid cancer metastasis

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with and without gadolinium contrast
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Infiltrative right temporal mass with atypical patchy enhancement pattern creating diagnostic uncertainty between primary glioma, metastasis, and lymphoma

optimal_actions.action: Obtain EEG to evaluate for subclinical seizure activity and characterize the reported motor event
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Generalized and focal slowing with equivocal sharp transients; insufficient for definitive localization

optimal_actions.action: Initiate anti-seizure medication (levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily) given the witnessed focal motor seizure and structural brain lesion
Order MR perfusion imaging to assess cerebral blood volume and distinguish primary glioma from metastasis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated rCBV in both enhancing and non-enhancing regions with elevated perilesional perfusion, confirming infiltrative high-grade glioma rather than metastasis

optimal_actions.action: Order MR spectroscopy for metabolic characterization of the lesion
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated choline, reduced NAA, lipid/lactate peaks in enhancing regions with metabolic abnormalities extending into non-enhancing tissue — pathognomonic for infiltrative glioma, excluding metastasis

optimal_actions.action: Order CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis to evaluate for thyroid cancer recurrence and other primary malignancies
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No evidence of thyroid cancer recurrence or other extracranial malignancy

optimal_actions.action: Initiate dexamethasone 4-8 mg IV for vasogenic edema and mass effect
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Improvement in perilesional edema, headache, and neurological symptoms

optimal_actions.action: Proceed with stereotactic biopsy with molecular profiling (IDH, MGMT, 1p/19q, TERT, EGFR) and immunohistochemistry (TTF-1, PAX8, GFAP) to definitively distinguish primary brain tumor from thyroid metastasis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, WHO grade 4 with methylated MGMT promoter; TTF-1 and PAX8 negative excluding thyroid origin

optimal_actions.action: Obtain functional MRI for pre-surgical language and motor mapping given right temporal location
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left-dominant language with mild bilateral representation; partial overlap of right hemispheric language co-activation with tumor margin

optimal_actions.action: Plan maximal safe surgical resection with awake craniotomy and language mapping, followed by Stupp protocol (concurrent temozolomide and radiation therapy)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Comprehensive surgical and adjuvant treatment plan established via multidisciplinary tumor board

critical_actions: Do not assume the intracranial mass is a thyroid cancer metastasis based solely on the cancer history and elevated thyroglobulin — advanced imaging and tissue biopsy are essential to differentiate primary brain tumor from metastasis
Order MR perfusion and MR spectroscopy to evaluate whether the lesion shows an infiltrative (glioma) or non-infiltrative (metastasis) pattern in perilesional tissue
Request immunohistochemistry for TTF-1 and PAX8 on biopsy tissue to definitively exclude metastatic thyroid carcinoma
Initiate anti-seizure medication after the witnessed focal motor seizure in the setting of a structural brain lesion
Request molecular profiling (IDH, MGMT, 1p/19q, TERT, EGFR) on biopsy to guide treatment planning — particularly MGMT methylation status for temozolomide response prediction
Perform metastatic workup (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis) to evaluate for thyroid cancer recurrence or other primary malignancies
contraindicated_actions: Treating the brain lesion as a presumed thyroid cancer metastasis with systemic thyroid cancer therapy (e.g., radioactive iodine, lenvatinib, sorafenib) without tissue confirmation — this would delay appropriate GBM treatment with potentially fatal consequences
Administering radioactive iodine therapy empirically for presumed thyroid metastasis without tissue diagnosis
Performing lumbar puncture in the setting of mass effect and midline shift due to risk of uncal herniation
Withholding corticosteroids in the presence of significant vasogenic edema and mass effect
Using enzyme-inducing anti-epileptic drugs (carbamazepine, phenytoin, phenobarbital) that would reduce temozolomide efficacy via CYP450 induction
Discharging the patient for outpatient follow-up given the large mass with mass effect and focal neurological deficits
key_reasoning_points: The prior thyroid follicular carcinoma history is the central confounding factor in this diagnostic puzzle — it creates a plausible explanation for the brain lesion as a metastasis, potentially leading to premature diagnostic closure

The mildly elevated thyroglobulin (3.2 ng/mL) is a deliberate red herring: after hemithyroidectomy (not total thyroidectomy), residual normal thyroid tissue can produce measurable thyroglobulin levels, and the value is within the range explained by residual tissue rather than necessarily indicating cancer recurrence

The atypical MRI appearance (infiltrative mass with patchy rather than ring enhancement, no classic central necrosis) creates additional diagnostic uncertainty — it does not look like a typical GBM, which may steer clinicians toward alternative diagnoses including metastasis

MR perfusion is a critical discriminating study: elevated rCBV in perilesional non-enhancing tissue is characteristic of infiltrative glioma and effectively excludes metastasis, which shows elevated perfusion only within the enhancing lesion

MR spectroscopy provides the second key discriminating study: metabolic abnormalities (elevated choline, reduced NAA) extending into non-enhancing tissue confirm infiltrative tumor spread, which is pathognomonic for primary diffuse glioma and incompatible with metastatic disease

The recent extensive medical workup (cardiology, gastroenterology) for nonspecific symptoms (dyspnea, chest discomfort, dysphagia) in retrospect may represent early constitutional symptoms or could be entirely unrelated — these red herrings add to the complexity of the case
The family history of breast and prostate cancer adds another layer of concern for metastatic disease from an undiagnosed primary, but CT body imaging is negative

TTF-1 and PAX8 immunohistochemistry on biopsy tissue is the definitive test to exclude thyroid carcinoma metastasis — an expert clinician should specifically request these stains given the clinical history

metadata.difficulty_description: Diagnostic puzzle where a prior history of thyroid follicular carcinoma and a mildly elevated thyroglobulin level create a compelling but incorrect narrative for brain metastasis. The atypical MRI appearance (infiltrative with patchy enhancement rather than classic ring-enhancing GBM) adds further diagnostic uncertainty. Advanced imaging (MR perfusion and spectroscopy) and tissue-based immunohistochemistry are required to definitively establish the correct diagnosis of primary glioblastoma.
